chapter_context.summary: The parable of the wedding banquet, paying taxes to Caesar, and the greatest commandment.

outline.text: The Parable of the Wedding Banquet
outline.explanation: This section focuses on The Parable of the Wedding Banquet (22:1-14), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Matthew.

outline.text: Paying the Imperial Tax and the Greatest Commandment
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Paying the Imperial Tax and the Greatest Commandment (22:15-46), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Matthew.

cultural_historical.title: Matthew's Jewish Audience
cultural_historical.text: Matthew primarily wrote to Jewish Christians, emphasizing Jesus as the long-awaited Messiah who fulfills Old Testament law and prophecy.

key_themes.title: Love for God and Neighbor
key_themes.description: The two greatest commandments summarize the Law and Prophets.

devotional.question: What does chapter 22 teach you about the character of Jesus?
How can you apply the teachings of this chapter to your daily life?

37.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:37 Jesus answers from the Shema (Deut 6:4-9), one of the core statements of God’s covenant with Israel (The first Hebrew word of Deut 6:4-9 is shema‘, “hear”).

1.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:1-14 In this parable, Israel, having repeatedly rejected God’s word in the past, rejects the Messiah and is judged as a result. In Israel’s place, God raised up the church (16:17-19; 21:43), of which righteousness is also expected (Rom 11:11-24).

2.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:2 The king represents God, whose son is Jesus (Gal 4:4; Heb 1:1-2). • The feast represents the Kingdom of the Messiah.

3.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:3-5 Those who were invited refers to the nation of Israel, who ignored God’s servants, the prophets (see study note on Matt 21:33-46).

### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
and sent forth his servants--representing all preachers of the Gospel. 

to call them that were bidden--here meaning the Jews, who were "bidden," from the first choice of them onwards through every summons addressed to them by the prophets to hold themselves in readiness for the appearing of their King. 

to the wedding--or the marriage festivities, when the preparations were all concluded. 

and they would not come--as the issue of the whole ministry of the Baptist, our Lord Himself, and His apostles thereafter, too sadly showed.

4.commentary:
### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
my oxen and my fatlings are killed, and all things are ready; come unto the marriage--This points to those Gospel calls after Christ's death, resurrection, ascension, and effusion of the Spirit, to which the parable could not directly allude, but when only it could be said, with strict propriety, "that all things were ready." Compare Co1 5:7-8, "Christ our Passover is sacrificed for us; therefore, let us keep the feast"; also Joh 6:51, "I am the living bread which came down from heaven: if any man eat of this bread, he shall live for ever: and the bread which I will give is My flesh, which I will give for the life of the world."

5.commentary:
### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
But they made light of it, and went their ways, one to his farm, another to his merchandise:

6.commentary:
### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
And the remnant took his servants, and entreated them spitefully--insulted them. 

and slew them--These are two different classes of unbelievers: the one simply indifferent; the other absolutely hostile--the one, contemptuous scorners; the other, bitter persecutors.

7.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:7 Jesus was alluding to the coming destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70 (see 23:37-39; 24:2).

### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
But when the king--the Great God, who is the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ. 

heard thereof, he was wroth--at the affront put both on His Son, and on Himself who had deigned to invite them. 

and he sent forth his armies--The Romans are here styled God's armies, just as the Assyrian is styled "the rod of His anger" (Isa 10:5), as being the executors of His judicial vengeance. 

and destroyed those murderers--and in what vast numbers did they do it! 

and burned up their city--Ah! Jerusalem, once "the city of the Great King" (Psa 48:2), and even up almost to this time (Mat 5:35); but now it is "their city"--just as our Lord, a day or two after this, said of the temple, where God had so long dwelt, "Behold your house is left unto you desolate" (Mat 23:38)! Compare Luk 19:43-44.

8.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:8 Those who reject God’s invitation aren’t worthy to enter his Kingdom (cp. 3:8; 10:13, 37-38).

### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
The wedding is ready, but they which were bidden were not worthy--for how should those be deemed worthy to sit down at His table who had affronted Him by their treatment of His gracious invitation?

9.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:9 everyone you see: God’s invitation is no longer restricted to the nation of Israel (see 28:16-20; cp. 10:5-6; 15:21-28).

### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
Go ye therefore into the highways--the great outlets and thoroughfares, whether of town or country, where human beings are to be found. 

and as many as ye shall find, bid to the marriage--that is, just as they are.

10.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:10 good and bad alike: See 13:24-50.

### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
So those servants went out into the highways, and gathered together all as many as they found, both bad and good--that is, without making any distinction between open sinners and the morally correct. The Gospel call fetched in Jews, Samaritans, and outlying heathen alike. Thus far the parable answers to that of "the Great Supper" (Luk 14:16, &c.). But the distinguishing feature of our parable is what follows:

11.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:11 The proper clothes correspond to spiritual fruit that demonstrates real faith (see 7:13-27).

### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
And when the king came in to see the guests--Solemn expression this, of that omniscient inspection of every professed disciple of the Lord Jesus from age to age, in virtue of which his true character will hereafter be judicially proclaimed! 

he saw there a man--This shows that it is the judgment of individuals which is intended in this latter part of the parable: the first part represents rather national judgment. 

which had not on a wedding garment--The language here is drawn from the following remarkable passage in Zep 1:7-8 : --"Hold thy peace at the presence of the Lord God; for the day of the Lord is at hand: for the Lord hath prepared a sacrifice, He hath bid His guests. And it shall come to pass in the day of the Lord's sacrifice, that I will punish the princes, and the king's children, and all such as are clothed with strange apparel." The custom in the East of presenting festival garments (see Gen 45:22; Kg2 5:22), even though nor clearly proved, Is certainly presupposed here. It undoubtedly means something which they bring not of their own--for how could they have any such dress who were gathered in from the highways indiscriminately?--but which they receive as their appropriate dress. And what can that be but what is meant by "putting on the Lord Jesus," as "THE LORD OUR RIGHTEOUSNESS?" (See Psa 45:13-14). Nor could such language be strange to those in whose ears had so long resounded those words of prophetic joy: "I will greatly rejoice in the Lord, my soul shall be joyful in my God; for He hath clothed me with the garments of salvation, He hath covered me with the robe of righteousness, as a bridegroom decketh himself with ornaments, and as a bride adorneth herself with her jewels" (Isa 61:10).

12.commentary:
### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
Friend, how camest thou in hither, not having a wedding garment? And he was speechless--being self-condemned.

13.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:13 Weeping and gnashing of teeth express the intense pain and sorrow that result from condemnation for sin and unbelief (see 8:12; 13:42, 50; 24:51; 25:30). • Outer darkness is a metaphor for eternal punishment.

### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
Then said the king to the servants--the angelic ministers of divine vengeance (as in Mat 13:41). 

Bind him hand and foot--putting it out of his power to resist. 

and take him away, and cast him into outer darkness--So Mat 8:12; Mat 25:30. The expression is emphatic--"the darkness which is outside." To be "outside" at all--or, in the language of Rev 22:15, to be "without" the heavenly city, excluded from its joyous nuptials and gladsome festivities--is sad enough of itself, without anything else. But to find themselves not only excluded from the brightness and glory and joy and felicity of the kingdom above, but thrust into a region of "darkness," with all its horrors, this is the dismal retribution here announced, that awaits the unworthy at the great day. 

there--in that region and condition. 

shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth. See on Mat 13:42.

14.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:14 While many people are called—they hear the invitation to the Kingdom—few are actually chosen by God and respond in faithful obedience to Jesus.

### Jamieson-Fausset-Brown
For many are called, but few are chosen--So Mat 19:30. See on Mat 20:16.




Next: Matthew Chapter 23

15.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:15-22 Roman taxation was a controversial issue in first-century Palestine (see also 17:24-27).

16.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:16 The Pharisees, strict nationalists who resented Roman rule, were normally at odds with the supporters of Herod. Here, the unlikely alliance arose from a mutual hatred of Jesus.

17.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:17 Is it right . . . or not? The question was designed to trap Jesus. If he answered no, he could be arrested for rebellion against Rome. If he answered yes, he could be accused of supporting Roman oppression.

18.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:18 You hypocrites! See study note on 23:13.

19.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:18 You hypocrites! See study note on 23:13.

20.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:20 The poll tax had to be paid using the denarius coin, which bore the picture and title of Caesar. Old Testament prohibitions against images (Exod 20:4; Lev 26:1; Deut 4:15-24) made the use of this coin controversial among Jews (see Josephus, War 2.9.2-3). Some Roman procurators apparently produced coins without images for use in Palestine in order not to offend the Jewish conscience.

21.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:21 Jesus outwitted his opponents by affirming the honor due to Caesar, while clearly differentiating it from the supreme honor and allegiance due to God. Loyalty to God does not necessarily entail disloyalty to the governing authorities. Jesus was acknowledging two dominions, Caesar’s and God’s, the latter having priority (Acts 4:19).

22.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:21 Jesus outwitted his opponents by affirming the honor due to Caesar, while clearly differentiating it from the supreme honor and allegiance due to God. Loyalty to God does not necessarily entail disloyalty to the governing authorities. Jesus was acknowledging two dominions, Caesar’s and God’s, the latter having priority (Acts 4:19).

23.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:23-33 The resurrection was an important issue of Jesus’ day, but the question was raised only to trap Jesus.
22:23 The Sadducees (see 3:7) described a situation involving levirate marriage (see Gen 38:6-11; Deut 25:5-6), which they considered proof that resurrection from the dead is not possible.

24.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:23-33 The resurrection was an important issue of Jesus’ day, but the question was raised only to trap Jesus.
22:23 The Sadducees (see 3:7) described a situation involving levirate marriage (see Gen 38:6-11; Deut 25:5-6), which they considered proof that resurrection from the dead is not possible.

### Tyndale Study Notes
22:23-33 The resurrection was an important issue of Jesus’ day, but the question was raised only to trap Jesus.
22:23 The Sadducees (see 3:7) described a situation involving levirate marriage (see Gen 38:6-11; Deut 25:5-6), which they considered proof that resurrection from the dead is not possible.

22:23 The Sadducees (see 3:7) described a situation involving levirate marriage (see Gen 38:6-11; Deut 25:5-6), which they considered proof that resurrection from the dead is not possible.

27.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes

22:23 The Sadducees (see 3:7) described a situation involving levirate marriage (see Gen 38:6-11; Deut 25:5-6), which they considered proof that resurrection from the dead is not possible.

28.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:23-33 The resurrection was an important issue of Jesus’ day, but the question was raised only to trap Jesus.
22:23 The Sadducees (see 3:7) described a situation involving levirate marriage (see Gen 38:6-11; Deut 25:5-6), which they considered proof that resurrection from the dead is not possible.

29.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:29 The power of God most likely refers to God’s ability to raise the dead.

30.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:30 like the angels in heaven: Jesus was not teaching genderlessness (i.e., androgyny) nor disparaging the divine order of marriage and sexuality; rather, he was affirming that people will be transformed into a glorious new existence (see 1 Cor 15:35-49; 2 Cor 5:1-5) in which aspects of the present order, such as marriage, will not be present.

31.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:31-32 Since God spoke of being the God of Abraham in the present tense, this proves that Abraham still lives and thus that there is a resurrection.

32.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:31-32 Since God spoke of being the God of Abraham in the present tense, this proves that Abraham still lives and thus that there is a resurrection.

33.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:31-32 Since God spoke of being the God of Abraham in the present tense, this proves that Abraham still lives and thus that there is a resurrection.

34.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:31-32 Since God spoke of being the God of Abraham in the present tense, this proves that Abraham still lives and thus that there is a resurrection.

35.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:31-32 Since God spoke of being the God of Abraham in the present tense, this proves that Abraham still lives and thus that there is a resurrection.

36.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:36 Since many strict Jews saw all commandments as equally binding, a careless response to this question could lead to the accusation of undermining the law of God.

38.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:37 Jesus answers from the Shema (Deut 6:4-9), one of the core statements of God’s covenant with Israel (The first Hebrew word of Deut 6:4-9 is shema‘, “hear”).

39.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:39 Love your neighbor as yourself: Lev 19:18; see also Rom 13:9; Gal 5:6, 14; Jas 2:8.

40.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:40 are based on: Jesus’ statement affirmed the unity and coherence of God’s will, as recorded in Scripture.

41.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:41-46 Having successfully defended himself against his opponents’ traps (21:23–22:40), Jesus questioned them as to how the Messiah can be called both the son of David and, at the same time, the Lord of David (Ps 110:1).

42.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:42 the son of David: See study note on Matt 9:27; see also 2 Sam 7:12-14; Pss 2:7-9; 110; Isa 11:1, 10; Jer 23:5-6.

43.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:42 the son of David: See study note on Matt 9:27; see also 2 Sam 7:12-14; Pss 2:7-9; 110; Isa 11:1, 10; Jer 23:5-6.

44.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:44 The quotation is from Ps 110:1, which is frequently cited by New Testament authors to describe Jesus (see Acts 2:34-35; 1 Cor 15:25; Heb 1:13; 2:8; 10:12-13; Rev 3:21).

45.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:45 how can the Messiah be his son? The answer is that Jesus is more than the son of David: He is the Son of God (14:33).

46.commentary:
### Tyndale Study Notes
22:46 No one could answer him because they had never thought the Messiah would be God as a human being in the flesh (see John 1:1-14).
